text.fullText: Opposing Provisions in EPA’s Proposed Rule That Reduce State Authority Under CWA §401 Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 Federal Register Vol. 91, No. 10 (Jan. 15, 2026) Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin: I submit these comments in strong opposition to several provisions of EPA’s proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, published January 15, 2026 (91 Fed. Reg. 2008). The proposed revisions would reduce state authority, narrow the scope of water quality review, and weaken environmental protections that Congress expressly granted to states under Clean Water Act (CWA) §401. My comments identify the specific provisions that diminish state authority and explain why they conflict with the statute, legislative history, and longstanding cooperative federalism principles. 1. Narrowing the Scope of Certification (40 CFR 121.3) EPA proposes to revise the scope of certification at 40 CFR 121.3, citing stakeholder concerns about the 2023 rule’s “scope of certification” and expressing intent to “align the regulations with the scope of the Clean Water Act”. This revision is deeply concerning because: • The proposal signals a shift away from the “activity as a whole” standard reaffirmed in 2023. • EPA frames the revision as responding to industry requests for “clarity and transparency” regarding scope—language historically used to justify narrowing state review authority. • The proposal risks reverting toward the 2020 “discharge-only” approach, which courts and states widely criticized as inconsistent with PUD No. 1 v. Washington. Impact: A narrowed scope would prevent states from evaluating impacts to flow, hydrology, habitat, sedimentation, and other physical and biological parameters—contrary to the CWA’s objective to protect the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of waters. 2. Restricting the Contents of Certification Decisions (40 CFR 121.7) EPA proposes to revise the contents of a certification decision at 40 CFR 121.7. This revision may reduce state authority by: • Limiting the types of conditions states may impose; • Narrowing the legal basis for state-law requirements; • Imposing new procedural hurdles that constrain state discretion. Impact: Section 401(d) authorizes states to include “any effluent limitations and other limitations” and “any other appropriate requirement of State law.” Any regulatory narrowing of this authority contradicts the statute’s plain text. 3. Weakening State Ability to Modify Certifications (40 CFR 121.10) EPA proposes to revise the modification process at 40 CFR 121.10 and remove elements added in 2023 . This reduces protections because: • States often need to modify certifications when new information emerges. • Restricting modification authority locks states into outdated or incomplete conditions. • Long-duration projects (pipelines, hydropower, mining) require adaptive management. Impact: Limiting modification authority undermines states’ ability to ensure ongoing compliance with water quality requirements. 4. Adding New Restrictions on Withdrawal and Resubmittal (40 CFR 121.6) EPA proposes to add new regulatory text on withdrawal and resubmittal of certification requests at 40 CFR 121.6. This is a significant rollback because: • Withdrawal/resubmittal is a critical tool that allows states to complete complex reviews without triggering automatic waiver. • Restricting this practice forces states into rushed decisions. • The proposal appears to respond to industry complaints rather than statutory requirements. Impact: Constraining withdrawal/resubmittal increases the likelihood of involuntary waivers, reducing state oversight and weakening environmental protections. 5. Removing Tribal “Treatment as a State” (TAS) Provision (40 CFR 121.11) EPA proposes to remove the TAS provision at 40 CFR 121.11 and rely on a different process under §303(c). This change may: • Delay Tribal access to §401 authority; • Create procedural barriers not faced by states; • Reduce protections for Tribal waters. Impact: Removing a direct regulatory pathway for Tribal certification authority undermines the CWA’s cooperative federalism framework. 6. Emphasis on “Efficiency,” “Predictability,” and “Transparency” EPA repeatedly states that the rule aims to increase “transparency, efficiency, and predictability” for certifying authorities and the regulated community. While these goals are reasonable, in practice they often serve as justification for: • Shorter timelines; • Narrower scope; • Reduced state discretion; • Greater federal preemption. Impact: Efficiency must not come at the expense of state authority or water quality protection. 7. Responding to Industry Requests to Narrow State Authority EPA acknowledges that the proposed revisions respond to industry stakeholder requests to revise the 2023 rule’s scope of certification. Impact: Rulemaking driven by industry pressure risks undermining the statutory role of states and Tribes as primary protectors of water quality. Conclusion For the reasons stated above, I strongly oppose the provisions of the proposed rule that reduce state authority under CWA §401. The Clean Water Act establishes a cooperative federalism framework in which states and Tribes play a central role in protecting water quality. Any regulatory revision that narrows state authority, limits the scope of review, restricts conditions, or increases the likelihood of waiver is inconsistent with the statute and undermines environmental protection. I urge EPA to: 1. Retain the “activity as a whole” scope of review; 2. Preserve broad state authority to impose conditions under §401(d); 3. Maintain flexible timelines and withdrawal/resubmittal practices; 4. Preserve modification authority; 5. Retain the TAS provision; 6. Ensure that revisions do not reduce state or Tribal ability to protect their waters. Sincerely, Ms. Patricia Ragland
